PRJ_04 · 系统影响 · 2023–2024

RotaCarbon

A solar-powered rotary activated-carbon flue-gas purification system for rural Chinese kitchens — born in Yueyang, Hunan, patented nationally, and deployed across five townships.

Context

Rural Revitalisation Project
Yueyang, Hunan Province

Role

Project Lead · Designer
Commercial Promotion

Tech Stack

Mechanical Design Solar Power Activated Carbon

Outcome

1× National Utility Patent
Deployed · 5 townships

01 · The Challenge

Wood smoke
has nowhere
to go.

In rural China, firewood remains the primary fuel source. Most farmhouses have a dedicated wood-burning kitchen — used for cooking, boiling water, heating, and smoking meat. These spaces are central to daily life.

Yet the majority of these kitchens suffer from chronic under-ventilation. Combustion smoke — carrying CO, VOCs, and particulate matter — accumulates indoors with no effective exit path. Prolonged exposure significantly raises the risk of asphyxiation, chronic poisoning, and respiratory disease for the families working inside them.

Commercial range hoods (¥2,000+) are financially inaccessible to low-income rural households. The problem is structural, not behavioural. It required a ground-up engineering response — one that could be built and maintained by the communities themselves.

¥1,000

Total device cost

50%

CO/VOCs reduction

5

Townships deployed

Extended application · street-food kitchen deployment scenario

Performance measured by professional laboratory instruments

02 · The Design

Three iterations.
One solution
that sticks.

The design was developed iteratively around three constraints: cost at half of a commercial range hood, self-sufficient solar power, and installation without professional labour. Each version addressed failures found in the field.

Target user & application

→

Target customer: low-income rural villagers who cook and smoke meat in close, poorly ventilated environments

→

Application: poorly ventilated kitchens and smokehouses

→

Functions: enhanced indoor ventilation · CO purification · VOC interception via activated carbon

→

Unique advantages: solar-powered · replaceable & reusable consumables · modular design using low-cost materials

Design iteration history

Version 1 · Concept Sketch

Initial system architecture: dual aluminium exhaust duct, activated carbon brick filter array, exhaust fan, solar battery pack. Established the core duct-fan-carbon logic.

Version 2 · Structural Sketch

Based on V1, the structure of rural kitchens was considered, with extended and movable chimneys. Inexpensive materials (such as PP, bricks, etc.) were used to enhance the applicability in rural areas.

Version 3 · Deployed Prototype

Final deployed version: aluminium alloy fan + exhaust head, PVC mid-duct, wood-frame rear section. Duct threaded through smoke-rack between floors. Carbon bricks (3mm, 5mm, 8mm aperture) in 2×2×3 wooden box array. Tested at grandmother's farmhouse.

Version 1 · system architecture sketch · solar + carbon + fan

Version 2 · kitchen structural installation sketch

Version 3 · final deployed design · carbon brick box detail

03 · The Testing

Data from the
field. Not the lab.

Version 3 was deployed in a real farmhouse in Yueyang — duct running from the ground-floor wood-burning kitchen through the smoke-rack, with 800 iodine-value activated carbon bricks (10×10×10cm) arranged in 2×2×3 layers at three pore sizes: 8mm, 5mm, and 3mm. CO and VOC concentrations were measured over 300 seconds under active fire conditions.

CO & VOCs concentration · device deployed (8mm bricks)

Both pollutants remain significantly below dangerous thresholds over 300s

CO adsorption · aperture comparison vs control

Smaller aperture = stronger CO adsorption; all brick sizes outperform control

VOC adsorption · aperture comparison vs control

Activated carbon shows exceptionally strong VOC purification; 3mm bricks most effective

Device cost breakdown · total ¥1,139

Carbon brick replacement (2.6%) and solar-only power keeps running cost near zero

Finding 1

Device significantly improves ventilation — indoor air quality maintained within the range suitable for physical labour throughout testing.

Finding 2

Smaller pore size correlates directly with stronger purification capacity. 3mm bricks achieved the best performance for both CO and VOCs.

Finding 3

Activated carbon is highly effective against VOCs but shows weaker CO adsorption — validating the dual role of ventilation + filtration in the system architecture.

04 · The Engineering · National Utility Patent

Rotary seal.
Intermittent flow.
Maximum contact.

The core innovation of RotaCarbon is its intermittent-flow mechanism: a servo-motor-driven incomplete bevel gear sequentially opens one pipe at a time, forcing flue gas to pause inside each activated-carbon tube before being released — dramatically increasing contact time and purification efficiency.

Fig. 1 · Full assembly · exhaust pipe (1), exhaust disc (2), pipes (3), inlet disc (4), inlet pipe (5), motor (14)

Fig. 2 · Inlet disc assembly · inlet disc (4), inlet pipe (5), fan (12), connection nozzles (11)

Fig. 3 · Pipe exterior · pipe body (3), shaft (8), driven bevel gear (7), torsion spring (9), air guide nozzle (10)

Fig. 4 · Pipe cross-section · seal wheel (6), activated carbon mesh (17), retaining ring (18), fan (7), spring (9)

Working principle · intermittent rotary filtration

01

Fan (12) draws flue gas from the stove into inlet pipe (5), distributing it through inlet disc (4) into multiple parallel pipes (3).

02

In default state, the seal wheel (6) closes each pipe — gas cannot flow. It dwells in contact with the activated carbon mesh (17), maximising adsorption.

03

Servo motor (14) drives an incomplete bevel gear (13) that sequentially meshes with each driven bevel gear (7), briefly rotating the seal wheel open — releasing treated gas into exhaust disc (2).

04

When the gear disengages, the torsion spring (9) returns the seal wheel to closed position. Cycle repeats — one pipe opens at a time, creating pulsed filtration.

05

Activated carbon meshes (17) are cylindrical, removable via inlet disc (4) detachment — field-replaceable without tools, keeping maintenance cost at 2.6% of total device cost.

Component index · patent reference

(1) Exhaust pipeFlue gas outlet to atmosphere
(2) Exhaust discCollects treated gas from all pipes
(3) Filter pipesMultiple, circumferentially distributed
(4) Inlet discDistributes incoming gas to all pipes
(5) Inlet pipe + (12) FanDraws smoke from stove
(6) Seal wheelDisc-shaped, normally closed
(7) Driven bevel gear + (9) Torsion springOpen/close mechanism per pipe
(13) Incomplete bevel gear + (14) Servo motorSequential actuation driver
(17) Activated carbon meshCylindrical, field-replaceable
(18) Retaining ringSecures carbon mesh in pipe
